critic_raw:
FamNet's test-time adaptation strategy uses exemplar objects to adapt the counting network to novel categories. The adaptation is performed using gradient updates on test images, which raises two concerns: (1) the adaptation is specific to each test image and does not generalize across images of the same category, requiring per-image adaptation overhead at inference time; (2) gradient updates at test time on a trained network can cause catastrophic forgetting or representation drift — the network's features for previously seen categories may degrade as the model adapts to each new test image.

The few-shot counting paradigm requires the model to generalize to categories not seen during training. The FSC-147 dataset introduced in the paper contains 147 categories, but the difficulty of generalization depends critically on how different the test categories are from training categories. If training and test categories have significant visual overlap (e.g., training includes 'cat' and test includes 'tiger'), the few-shot generalization claim is weaker than if the categories are truly disjoint in visual appearance. The category split used for training vs. testing is not described.

The comparison against 'object detectors and few-shot counting approaches' as baselines may be unfair if the detectors are not given the same exemplar information. Object detectors trained on specific categories are disadvantaged when counting novel categories without re-training — using them as baselines for a few-shot method essentially compares specialized single-category detectors against a multi-category few-shot method, which does not test whether the few-shot approach adds value over few-shot object detectors that also use exemplars.
all_issues_raised: Per-image test-time adaptation incurs per-image overhead and risks catastrophic forgetting or representation drift across test images.
Category split for train/test is not described; visual overlap between training and test categories would weaken the few-shot generalization claim.
Baseline object detectors are category-specific and not given exemplar information; the comparison may not test whether the few-shot approach adds value over few-shot detection methods.

round_transcripts.critic: Test-time adaptation per image is computationally expensive and risks representation drift. Category train/test split is undescribed; visual overlap would weaken generalization claims. Baseline comparison against category-specific detectors may not be a fair test of the few-shot approach.
round_transcripts.defender:
Test-time adaptation in FamNet is not a full network update — it updates only a small subset of parameters (or a dedicated adaptation module) using a few gradient steps on the exemplar-query relationships. The catastrophic forgetting risk is mitigated because the base network weights that handle generic visual feature extraction are not modified; only the adaptation component is updated. The per-image overhead is bounded by the number of gradient steps, which is a fixed small constant. This is standard practice in model-agnostic meta-learning settings.

On category splits: the FSC-147 dataset uses a category-disjoint split where training and test categories do not overlap. This is the standard protocol for evaluating generalization to novel categories and ensures the few-shot claim is tested under the appropriate conditions. The description's omission of this detail does not imply the split is inappropriate.

On baselines: the comparison against category-specific detectors is included to establish the performance gap when no few-shot capability is available — this motivates the few-shot counting problem. The comparison against few-shot counting methods is the primary performance comparison. Both types of baselines serve different purposes: one establishes the problem motivation, the other establishes the state-of-the-art comparison.

round_transcripts.critic:
The test-time adaptation response is substantive: if only an adaptation component is updated (not base feature extraction weights), catastrophic forgetting risk is substantially reduced. The meta-learning framing is appropriate. This concern is largely resolved.

On category splits: the defender asserts the split is category-disjoint, which resolves the concern if accurate. This is standard protocol for few-shot evaluation and is plausible for a paper introducing a new benchmark.

On baselines: the dual-purpose baseline explanation is reasonable — motivational baselines versus competitive baselines serve different functions. The concern about whether any few-shot detection methods with exemplar input are compared is the residual question.
round_transcripts.defender: The few-shot detection baseline concern is reasonable: if state-of-the-art few-shot object detectors that also use exemplar inputs exist and are not compared, FamNet's advantage over exemplar-based methods is not established. Whether the paper includes such baselines is not clear from the description. If no exemplar-based detection baselines are compared, the comparison scope is incomplete for positioning FamNet in the few-shot detection literature.

all_issues_adjudicated: Whether few-shot object detection methods with exemplar input are compared is unclear; if absent, FamNet's advantage over exemplar-based baselines is not established.
